definition: Command Line Interface. A text-based user interface used to navigate and run programs on an operating system directly using text commands.
A distributed version control system used to track changes in source code during software development, enabling branching and merging.
Document Object Model. The programming interface for HTML documents, allowing JavaScript to dynamically manipulate the structure, style, and content of a web page.
The core mechanism in JavaScript that handles asynchronous operations by continuously checking the call stack and executing queued callbacks when the stack is empty.
An asynchronous, event-driven backend JavaScript runtime environment that executes JavaScript code outside a web browser.
A minimal, industry-standard routing and middleware framework for Node.js used to build robust web and mobile APIs.
A modern, high-performance Python web framework for building APIs, heavily favored in the industry for serving AI and ML models efficiently.
A NoSQL database that stores data in flexible, JSON-like document structures, mapping perfectly to JavaScript objects.
A powerful, open-source object-relational database system (SQL) known for strict schemas, complex joins, and enterprise reliability.
A next-generation ORM (Object-Relational Mapper) for Node.js and TypeScript that automatically generates strict data types based on database schemas.
JSON Web Token. A compact, URL-safe string used to represent claims securely between a client and server, predominantly used for stateless user authentication.
A high-performance build system for JavaScript and TypeScript monorepos, allowing frontend and backend code to live in one repository and build concurrently.
A blazing-fast, all-in-one JavaScript runtime, bundler, test runner, and package manager written in Zig, acting as a drop-in replacement for Node.js.
A declarative JavaScript library for building user interfaces using component-based architecture and a Virtual DOM.
The industry-standard React framework that provides Server-Side Rendering (SSR), static site generation, and advanced file-system routing capabilities.
An open-source machine learning framework heavily used for deep learning research and production, built around multidimensional Tensors.
A multi-dimensional array containing elements of a single data type, serving as the fundamental mathematical data structure in machine learning algorithms.
Convolutional Neural Network. A class of deep neural networks highly effective at extracting features and analyzing visual imagery.
Recurrent Neural Network. A class of neural networks historically designed to recognize patterns in sequences of data, such as text or time series.
The core mathematical mechanism of Transformers that processes input in parallel, using Queries, Keys, and Values to weigh the contextual importance of different words in a sequence simultaneously.
An open-source ecosystem, library (transformers), and hub providing tools, datasets, and pre-trained models for state-of-the-art machine learning.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation. An architecture that provides an 'external memory' to LLMs by fetching relevant context from a vector database before generating an answer.
A specialized database (e.g., ChromaDB, Pinecone) designed to store and execute similarity searches on high-dimensional vectors (text embeddings).
A communications protocol providing full-duplex, bidirectional channels over a single TCP connection, essential for real-time applications and streaming AI tokens.
Web Real-Time Communication. A technology enabling direct peer-to-peer audio, video, and data streaming between web browsers without routing media through a central server.
Model Context Protocol. An open standard allowing developers to build universal tool APIs that autonomous AI agents can securely plug into and execute.
Low-Rank Adaptation. A Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) technique that allows training massive AI models quickly and cheaply on limited GPU hardware.
A platform that uses OS-level virtualization to deliver software in isolated, highly reproducible packages called containers.
An open-source orchestration system for automating the deployment, load balancing, scaling, and management of thousands of containerized applications.
A high-performance universal RPC framework using Protocol Buffers to enable lightning-fast, highly compressed binary communication between microservices.
A high-throughput and memory-efficient LLM inference and serving engine utilizing PagedAttention to eliminate KV Cache bottlenecks.
A framework for building stateful, multi-agent AI systems, allowing developers to define cyclical graphs and state machines rather than simple linear chains.
Direct Preference Optimization. A mathematical technique used to align an AI model's behavior directly with human preferences without needing complex reward models.
